title: PolyNorm: Few-Shot LLM-Based Text Normalization for Text-to-Speech
authors: Michel Wong, Ali Alshehri, Sophia Kao, Haotian He (Apple)
venue: arXiv preprint (v1, 2025-11-05); comments field per abs-page metadata: EMNLP 2025 Industry Track (not independently re-derived from HTML body, so reported per WebFetch metadata only — see paper's own 'Discrepancies vs. expected citation' note). cs.CL, cs.LG.
claim: The paper claims that prompt-based, few-shot LLM text normalization (PolyNorm) can replace traditional rule-based (WFST) TN systems, achieving consistent Word Error Rate (WER) reductions across eight typologically diverse languages compared to a production-grade rule-based baseline (Apple Siri's system), with substantially less engineering effort. It also claims a reusable contribution: a language-agnostic, LLM-assisted pipeline for automatic multilingual data curation/evaluation (PolyNorm-Benchmark), released for further research.
novelty: The authors present two contributions relative to prior work: (1) extending prior English/GPT-only few-shot TN work (Zhang et al. 2024, 'A chat about boring problems') to a genuinely multilingual, language-agnostic setting across 8 languages with a unified, structured 3-part prompt (Instruction Prompt + ICL examples + Target Input) that only varies the localized ICL examples (plus one Japanese-specific supplementary prompt for katakana output); (2) a scalable, language-agnostic automatic data-curation/evaluation pipeline (PolyNorm-Benchmark) built by using DeepSeek-R1 to synthesize normalization pairs across 27 categories per language, then expert-reviewed/corrected, released publicly as a new multilingual TN benchmark to avoid the poor quality of machine-translated ground truth they found unusable.

protocol.description: Each test instance is a single independent few-shot prompt call to the LLM (GPT-4o-mini or GPT-4o): the model receives (1) a standardized English Instruction Prompt defining the normalization task and listing 27ish categories plus several house rules (acronym spell-out policy, punctuation-to-words rules, compound/filename segmentation, file-extension pronunciation rules — quoted verbatim in Appendix A), (2) a fixed set of curated in-context learning (ICL) examples (80-100 high-quality machine-translated Kestrel + DeepSeek synthetic examples per language, excluded from the benchmark itself, 'each normalized and validated by experts,' §3.1), and (3) the Target Unnormalized Input to normalize. The model outputs one normalized string per input in one forward pass; there is no multi-turn interaction or iterative feedback loop at inference/scoring time. NOTE: an offline iterative 'Error Analysis and Hillclimbing Iteration' loop (§3.2) was used by the researchers themselves, across development, to refine the ICL example set and prompt over iterations (Table 5 'Iteration 2' vs Table 6 'Iteration 3' for GPT-4o) — this is prompt engineering/dataset curation done by the authors between benchmark runs, not a per-instance interactive protocol the model itself experiences.
protocol.observation_content: Per call, the model sees: the full Instruction Prompt text (quoted verbatim in Appendix A: 'You are an accurate text normalizer for {locale}...' plus the category list and 'Some important rules' bullet list), the fixed localized ICL demonstration examples (count 80-100 per language, per §3.1), and the single target unnormalized input string. It does not see any reference/gold answer, scoring feedback, or prior model outputs — this is a one-shot (per-instance) few-shot-prompted generation, not an interactive/graded-feedback loop.

representations.summary: The paper does not run a controlled ablation over input/output representation formats (e.g., JSON vs plain string, different serializations of the same instruction). The main representational variable studied is language/locale (8 languages, same unified prompt structure, only ICL examples and instructions localized), and, for Japanese specifically, an added supplementary prompt to force katakana output (mentioned in §3.1 and §5.2) to resolve homograph ambiguity. Non-whitespace languages (Chinese, Japanese) are scored with Character Error Rate instead of WER 'to avoid conflating tokenization errors with true normalization errors' (§4). No systematic comparison of alternative prompt formats/orderings or serialization styles is reported; the paper states the prompt format itself was fixed/unified across languages ('We use a unified prompt format across eight languages, varying only the localized examples,' §3.1).

models.name: GPT-4o-mini
strategies: few-shot prompting with curated in-context learning (ICL) examples
structured 3-part instruction prompt (task instruction + ICL demos + target input)
models.key_results: Table 2: WER (%) / BLEU (%) per language — German 7.18/78.35, American English 6.60/85.02, Mexican Spanish 11.19/62.49, French 7.70/79.84, Italian 8.33/72.81, Lithuanian 10.54/64.36 (worse WER than baseline's 10.04), Mandarin Chinese 6.65/79.04 (CER), Japanese 10.78/68.32 (CER).

models.name: GPT-4o
strategies: few-shot prompting with curated ICL examples
iterative prompt/ICL refinement across development iterations (§3.2; Table 5 = Iteration 2, Table 6 = Iteration 3, both for GPT-4o only)
models.key_results: Table 2 (final/best iteration): WER (%) / BLEU (%) — German 4.17/84.92, American English 4.28/89.85, Mexican Spanish 7.69/71.90, French 5.65/86.18, Italian 4.56/84.96, Lithuanian 6.99/73.96, Mandarin Chinese 5.05/83.11 (CER), Japanese 7.88/77.89 (CER). Text states 'GPT-4o achieves a WER ranging from 4.17% to 7.88% for all languages' (§4). Appendix D shows the hillclimbing trajectory: Table 5 (Iteration 2) overall WER per language (e.g. German 4.24, English 5.23, Japanese 12.32) vs Table 6 (Iteration 3, final) overall WER (German 4.17, English 4.28, Japanese 7.88) — improvement from iterative ICL-example refinement, esp. large for Japanese (12.32 to 7.88) and Lithuanian (12.22 to 6.99).

models.name: Baseline (Apple Siri production rule-based/WFST TN system)
strategies: rule-based, not an LLM; included as the comparison baseline, not tuned to PolyNorm-Benchmark
models.key_results: Table 2: WER (%) / BLEU (%) — German 10.74/60.83, American English 9.84/70.84, Mexican Spanish 11.92/55.74, French 9.72/69.02, Italian 15.02/55.14, Lithuanian 10.04/67.12, Mandarin Chinese 11.36/69.63, Japanese 17.49/47.76. Per-category breakdown in Appendix C, Table 4 (27 categories overall average per language, plus 7 selected categories: Address, Legal Ref., Currencies, Math. Expr., Musical Not., Phone Num., Sports Scores).

headline_numbers: GPT-4o WER range 4.17%-7.88% across all 8 languages, vs. baseline WER range 9.72%-17.49% (Table 2, §4)
GPT-4o-mini WER range 6.60%-11.19% (worse than baseline only for Lithuanian: 10.54% vs baseline 10.04%) (Table 2)
Baseline WER worst on Japanese (17.49%) and Italian (15.02%); best on French (9.72%) and American English (9.84%) (Table 2)
GPT-4o iterative refinement (Appendix D): Japanese overall WER dropped from 12.32% (Iteration 2, Table 5) to 7.88% (Iteration 3, Table 6); Lithuanian dropped from 12.22% to 6.99%
ICL example set size: 80-100 curated examples per language, excluded from the benchmark itself (§3.1)
Benchmark size: 27 categories x 20 examples = 540 high-quality data points per language, across 8 languages (§2)
failure_modes: The paper defines a normalization error as any output deviating from the expected conventional spoken form, giving examples: rendering 'Dr' as 'Doctor' instead of 'Drive' in an address, or normalizing an ambiguous time like '12:30' incorrectly (§3). §3.2 states common LLM errors included 'incorrect numeral expansions and inconsistent handling of language-specific formats such as date and currency,' addressed via iterative ICL-example refinement. Appendix B (Table 3) gives concrete baseline (rule-based, not LLM) failure examples across languages/categories: German address compound-number-word segmentation error ('fünfundvierzig' [forty-five, one word] incorrectly split into 'fünf und vierzig' [five and forty]), English legal-reference acronym not expanded, Mexican Spanish phone number read as a full number instead of digit-by-digit, French sports score read with wrong dash convention ('moins' instead of 'à'), Italian currency symbol not expanded (CHF left as letters), Lithuanian math expression mis-verbalized copula, Chinese musical notation symbol dropped, Japanese currency particle error. The paper also notes categories that pose general difficulty for rule-based systems: domain-specific unpredictable constructs (URLs/usernames) and context-dependent dash semantics (sports score 'to' vs. phone-number digit-by-digit) (§4). Limitations (§9): 'the model's performance is highly dependent on the quality and representativeness of data used as in-context learning examples'; categories in ICL examples 'may not capture the full range of normalization needs across different languages and varieties'; 'the model may still struggle with edge cases or uncommon linguistic patterns' even with expert-reviewed ICL examples.
one_shot_vs_multistep: At inference/evaluation time, every instance is normalized in a single forward pass given a fixed few-shot prompt (instruction + ICL examples + target input) — there is no per-instance interactive/multi-turn correction loop the model participates in (protocol is single-shot few-shot prompting, per §3 and Appendix A). The only 'multi-step' element is an offline, author-driven hillclimbing loop across development (§3.2): researchers manually inspect error patterns between iterations, revise/add ICL examples, and re-run the full one-shot evaluation — captured as discrete 'Iteration 2' (Table 5) vs 'Iteration 3' (Table 6) benchmark snapshots for GPT-4o, showing WER improving with better-curated examples (e.g., Japanese 12.32% to 7.88%). This is prompt/example curation between runs, not an interactive session the model itself experiences, so the paper's conclusion is that few curated demonstrations (not multi-turn dialogue) are what drives one-shot accuracy up.

relation_to_candidate.position: PolyNorm's protocol is single-shot per-instance (fixed few-shot prompt in, one normalized string out, scored automatically against a reference string via WER/BLEU/CER) — the same basic single-forward-pass, single-string-output shape c11 uses, but the verifier differs: PolyNorm's WER/BLEU/CER are soft, edit-distance/n-gram-overlap-based metrics with partial credit, not c11's 0/1 whole-string exact match against a deterministic reference function. PolyNorm observes natural, real-world normalization conventions per locale (learned from a small set of localized ICL demonstrations); it does not invent a house convention that deliberately deviates from a public standard the way c11 does (c11's rules are adversarially constructed to differ from RFC 8785 JCS / ISO dates / standard slugify). PolyNorm's demonstrations are drawn from the same distribution the model will be tested on (in-domain, real conventions), whereas c11 explicitly requires the model to infer or be given invented, non-standard rules and requires table-width to exceed demo/minibatch coverage to prevent copy-from-context.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both tasks are single-forward-pass text-to-canonical-string transformations scored automatically; both include date, number/cardinal, phone/telephone, currency, and other structured-field normalization as explicit category types (PolyNorm's 27 categories, e.g., Date, Telephone, Currency, Cardinal, Ordinal — Table 1 — substantially overlap with the field types c11's example instances draw on, e.g., dates, phone numbers). Both rely on few curated in-context examples to convey a convention (PolyNorm's localized ICL set of 80-100 examples; c11's demo sets, which the c11 dossier explicitly worries could achieve 'full-coverage copy-from-context' if too comprehensive). Both papers/candidates treat 'the convention isn't standard/obvious, so it must be learned from examples or instructions' as the core difficulty driver.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Verifier: PolyNorm scores with soft metrics (WER/BLEU/CER) that give partial credit for near-misses; c11 scores 0/1 exact match with no partial credit for a single wrong rule. (2) Convention source: PolyNorm's normalization targets follow real linguistic/locale conventions (documented, if inconsistently, in existing style guides and native-speaker intuition) that the model may have partially seen in pretraining; c11's house rules are invented specifically to be unlike any standard the model could have memorized, closing the contamination/prior-exposure gap PolyNorm cannot close. (3) Iteration: PolyNorm's few-shot examples are refined across iterations by the researchers offline based on aggregate error analysis (§3.2); c11's design intentionally caps demo-set/minibatch coverage below full table width so the optimization loop (e.g., GEPA) cannot simply transcribe all rules from one batch. (4) Output granularity/scoring surface: PolyNorm's error definition and metrics operate at the word/character level across a full spoken-form sentence (edit-distance-tolerant); c11's output is a single exact canonical string (e.g., canonical JSON, a slug) where any single deviation anywhere fails the whole example — a much less forgiving surface.
relation_to_candidate.what_c11_would_add: c11 would add: (a) a hard, no-partial-credit exact-match verifier instead of soft WER/BLEU/CER, isolating whether the model can execute an entire deterministic rule chain without any slip, not just get 'close'; (b) invented, standard-deviating conventions rather than natural-language locale conventions, removing the possibility that pretraining exposure to real style guides inflates apparent few-shot competence; (c) an explicit, controllable dial on demo/table coverage ratio (table-width > minibatch/demo width) to probe copy-from-context vs. genuine rule-induction — a design axis PolyNorm's fixed, always-inclusive ICL curation does not test; (d) per-rule stratified scoring so an aggregate accuracy number can be decomposed into which individual house rule failed, versus PolyNorm's category-level (not sub-rule-level) WER breakdown (Table 4-6).

corpus_claim: PolyNorm (Wong et al., 2025) is evidence that date/phone/number normalization is a live LLM few-shot task; cited for c11's house-style normalizer family.
corpus_claim_verdict: supported, with a clarification. The paper does directly demonstrate that few-shot-prompted LLMs (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o) can perform date/phone/currency/cardinal/ordinal normalization (all explicit categories in Table 1, e.g., Date, Telephone, Currency, Cardinal, Ordinal, Fraction, Time) using only a small number of curated in-context examples (80-100 per language, §3.1), achieving lower WER than a production rule-based baseline (Table 2). This substantiates 'date/phone/number normalization is a live LLM few-shot task.' The clarification needed: PolyNorm's normalization targets are real, natural locale conventions (not invented house rules that deliberately deviate from a standard), and its verifier is soft (WER/BLEU/CER, partial credit) rather than exact-match — so the paper is evidence for the general feasibility and few-shot-learnability of this category of task, but not direct evidence for c11's specific stress point (whether models can execute an invented, standard-deviating, all-or-nothing exact-match convention). The c11 candidate page itself already frames the citation correctly this way ("evidence that date/phone/number normalization is a live LLM few-shot task" — not as an exact-match precedent), so the claim as stated is accurately scoped and supported by the paper.
baseline_relevant: Instance/benchmark scale: 27 categories x 20 examples = 540 test data points per language across 8 languages (§2); separate ICL example pool of 80-100 curated examples per language, excluded from the benchmark (§3.1).

Prompt format that mattered: a fixed 3-part structure (Instruction Prompt + ICL examples + Target Input) used uniformly across languages, varying only localized examples, with one added supplementary prompt for Japanese katakana output (§3.1) — suggests format itself was not the swing factor; example curation/localization was.

Metrics used: WER and BLEU for whitespace languages; CER substituted for WER in non-whitespace languages (Chinese, Japanese) 'to avoid conflating tokenization errors with true normalization errors' (§4) — a candidate consideration if c11's baseline needs a partial-credit companion metric alongside exact match.

Observed floor/ceiling: rule-based baseline WER floor-to-ceiling range 9.72%-17.49% across languages (Table 2); best LLM (GPT-4o) achieved 4.17%-7.88% after iterative refinement (Table 2, Table 6) — demonstrating that even well-tuned production rule systems have a nontrivial residual error rate humans/verifiers must tolerate, relevant context for c11's own note that its true ceiling for computation-heavy rules may sit in a 70-90% band.

Iteration/hillclimbing effect size: for GPT-4o, per-language WER improved substantially between Iteration 2 (Table 5) and Iteration 3 (Table 6), e.g., Japanese 12.32% -> 7.88%, Lithuanian 12.22% -> 6.99%, via manually revising/adding ICL examples after error analysis (§3.2) — evidence that demo-set curation quality/coverage strongly swings accuracy, directly relevant to c11's demo/table-coverage-ratio design concern.

Data-release/contamination status: PolyNorm-Benchmark is synthetically generated via DeepSeek-R1 then expert-reviewed, and publicly released on GitHub (https://github.com/apple/ml-speech-polynorm-bench, fn2) — i.e., a released, potentially futurely-contaminated benchmark, unlike c11's design goal of an infinite regenerable/contamination-proof pool.

Baseline system opacity: the rule-based production baseline is proprietary; 'we cannot release code or provide external access' (§4) — the paper only reports its scores, not its internals, which limits how much the baseline numbers can be used to calibrate an open, from-scratch reference canonicalizer the way c11 needs.
quotes: "We adopt a few-shot prompting strategy using ICL examples to guide the LLM in performing text normalization across all languages." (§3, Methodology)

"Our prompt design follows a structured, step-by-step and language-agnostic format composed of three components: 1) Instruction Prompt... 2) In-Context Learning Examples... and 3) Target Unnormalized Input." (§3)
"We use a unified prompt format across eight languages, varying only the localized examples—except for Japanese, where a supplementary prompt guides katakana output." (§3.1)
"GPT-4o achieves a WER ranging from 4.17% to 7.88% for all languages." (§4, Results)
"For non-whitespace languages (Chinese and Japanese), we report Character Error Rate instead of WER to avoid conflating tokenization errors with true normalization errors." (§4)

"We analyzed discrepancies between expert-verified development sets and LLM outputs from GPT models to identify systematic weaknesses. Common errors included incorrect numeral expansions and inconsistent handling of language-specific formats such as date and currency." (§3.2)

"the model's performance is highly dependent on the quality and representativeness of data used as in-context learning examples... the model may still struggle with edge cases or uncommon linguistic patterns." (§9, Limitations)
"Because it is proprietary, we cannot release code or provide external access [to the baseline]... It is important to note that the baseline predates our benchmark and was not tuned to it." (§4)

corrections: failure_modes: the German/Address baseline error example (Table 3) was mischaracterized as a 'digit-grouping error.' The actual error is a compound-number-word segmentation error: the baseline splits the single German compound numeral 'fünfundvierzig' (forty-five) into two separate words 'fünf und vierzig' (five and forty), not a digit-grouping issue. Corrected in the failure_modes field.
